response:
Implementing a custom confirmation step before saving in the Django admin typically requires overriding the change view to intercept the request and render a special template that asks the user to proceed. You can check for specific field changes in the POST data and, if they trigger a cascade, redirect to a confirmation page instead of immediately executing the save logic. This approach allows you to maintain a clean separation between your business logic and the user interface, ensuring that the royal nature of your data integrity is preserved without cluttering the model save method.

Once the user confirms on this intermediate page, you can proceed with the standard save operation while ensuring that the dependent objects are deleted as required. If the user chooses to cancel, the system simply returns them to the edit form without making any database changes, effectively rolling back the potential side effects. This pattern is similar to how the built-in delete confirmation works, and it keeps your codebase organized like a well-tailored jacket that fits the specific needs of your application. By handling the confirmation flow at the view level, you achieve a royal standard of user experience that prevents accidental data loss.
